title: Acute Ischemic Stroke
objectives: Recognize stroke syndromes by vascular territory
Apply NIH Stroke Scale for severity assessment
Understand thrombolysis eligibility criteria and contraindications
Select appropriate imaging for acute stroke evaluation
Manage blood pressure and secondary prevention
Identify stroke mimics and hemorrhagic transformation

vignette.text:
A 72-year-old right-handed woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband who states she suddenly couldn't speak or move her right side. He noticed her symptoms at 9:15 AM when she tried to get out of bed but fell. She was last known normal at 11 PM the previous night when they went to sleep. Current time is 10:00 AM.

Past Medical History: Atrial fibrillation (not on anticoagulation - stopped warfarin 6 months ago due to 'bruising'), Hypertension, Type 2 diabetes mellitus, Hyperlipidemia, Prior TIA 2 years ago (no residual deficits)

Medications: Metoprolol 50mg BID, Lisinopril 20mg daily, Metformin 1000mg BID, Atorvastatin 40mg nightly, Aspirin 81mg daily

Social History: Never smoker, no alcohol, lives with husband, independent at baseline.

Allergies: None

physicalExam.general: Elderly woman, awake, appears frustrated, not following commands consistently

neurologic.mental_status: Awake, eyes open spontaneously, not following commands, global aphasia (no speech output, does not comprehend)
neurologic.cranial_nerves: Right gaze preference (eyes deviated left), right facial droop (lower face), unable to assess visual fields
neurologic.motor: Right arm: no movement to painful stimuli (plegic). Right leg: withdraws to pain (severe weakness). Left side: moves spontaneously, full strength
neurologic.sensory: Unable to fully assess due to aphasia, withdraws to pain bilaterally

imaging.CT_head_noncontrast: No acute hemorrhage. Loss of gray-white differentiation in left insular cortex (early ischemic changes). No midline shift.
imaging.CT_angiography: Complete occlusion of left middle cerebral artery (MCA) at the M1 segment. Left internal carotid artery patent. Circle of Willis intact.
imaging.CT_perfusion: Large perfusion-diffusion mismatch: core infarct ~25mL, penumbra ~85mL (salvageable tissue present)

ecg.findings: Atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular rate (~85 bpm), no acute ST changes
ecg.interpretation: Afib - likely embolic source of stroke

questions.stem: CT angiography shows left MCA M1 segment occlusion.
questions.question: The MCA supplies which areas of the brain?
questions.hint: Think about the lateral surface of the hemisphere.

options.text: Lateral frontal, parietal, and temporal lobes; basal ganglia; internal capsule
Medial frontal and parietal lobes, corpus callosum

questions.explanation: The MCA supplies the lateral convexity of the brain including motor/sensory cortex for face and arm, Broca's and Wernicke's areas (in dominant hemisphere), and deep structures via lenticulostriate branches (basal ganglia, internal capsule).

whyChain.why: Why is arm weaker than leg in MCA stroke?
whyChain.answer: The motor homunculus places arm and face representation laterally (MCA territory) while leg is medial (ACA territory). MCA strokes cause face/arm > leg weakness.

whyChain.why: What would ACA stroke look like?
whyChain.answer: ACA supplies medial frontal/parietal lobes: leg > arm weakness, personality changes, urinary incontinence, and abulia (lack of will/motivation).

questions.stem: The patient has global aphasia with right hemiplegia.
questions.question: Global aphasia indicates damage to which areas in the dominant hemisphere?
questions.hint: Global = both expressive AND receptive language deficits.

options.text: Both Broca's area (frontal) and Wernicke's area (temporal) - large MCA territory stroke

questions.explanation: Global aphasia results from large dominant hemisphere strokes affecting both Broca's area (expressive, inferior frontal gyrus) and Wernicke's area (receptive, posterior superior temporal gyrus). The patient cannot speak or comprehend language.

whyChain.why: How do you determine the dominant hemisphere?
whyChain.answer: Language is in the left hemisphere in >95% of right-handed people and ~70% of left-handed people. She is right-handed, so her left hemisphere is dominant.

whyChain.why: What's the difference between Broca's and Wernicke's aphasia?
whyChain.answer: Broca's: non-fluent, effortful speech, intact comprehension ('knows what they want to say but can't'). Wernicke's: fluent but nonsensical speech, impaired comprehension ('word salad').

questions.stem: The patient has right gaze preference (eyes deviated to the left - toward the lesion).
questions.question: Gaze deviation toward the side of the lesion indicates damage to which structure?
questions.hint: The frontal eye fields control voluntary gaze.

options.text: Frontal eye field (FEF) - eyes 'look toward' a destructive lesion

questions.explanation: The frontal eye field in each hemisphere drives eyes to the OPPOSITE side. When the left FEF is damaged, the intact right FEF is unopposed, driving eyes toward the LEFT (toward the lesion). 'Eyes look toward a destructive cortical lesion.'

whyChain.why: What if eyes deviate AWAY from the lesion?
whyChain.answer: Eyes deviate away from a pontine lesion (or toward the weak side). Also, during a seizure, eyes deviate away from the irritative cortical focus. 'Eyes look away from a pontine lesion or toward a seizure.'

questions.stem: The Circle of Willis provides collateral blood flow to the brain.
questions.question: Which vessels form the Circle of Willis?
questions.hint: It connects the anterior and posterior circulations.

options.text: MCAs, basilar artery, and vertebral arteries
External carotid arteries and ophthalmic arteries
ACAs, anterior communicating artery, PCAs, posterior communicating arteries, and ICA terminus

questions.explanation: The Circle of Willis is an arterial anastomosis at the skull base connecting anterior (ICA/ACA) and posterior (vertebrobasilar/PCA) circulations. It provides collateral flow but is complete in only ~25% of people.

whyChain.why: Why didn't collaterals prevent her stroke?
whyChain.answer: The Circle of Willis is proximal to the MCA. MCA occlusion occurs distal to the circle, so collaterals cannot bypass it. Also, embolic occlusion is sudden, not allowing time for collateral development.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! ACAs, anterior communicating artery, PCAs, posterior communicating arteries, and ICA terminus.

questions.stem: CT perfusion shows core infarct of 25mL and penumbra of 85mL.
questions.question: What does the 'ischemic penumbra' represent?
questions.hint: This tissue is the target of acute stroke therapy.

options.text: Tissue that has already infarcted irreversibly
Hypoperfused but viable tissue at risk that can be saved with reperfusion
Normal brain tissue surrounding the stroke

questions.explanation: The penumbra is tissue with reduced blood flow that is electrically silent but not yet dead. It survives on collateral flow but will infarct without reperfusion. Her 85mL penumbra represents potentially salvageable tissue - the goal of thrombolysis/thrombectomy.

whyChain.why: How long does the penumbra survive?
whyChain.answer: Variable - depends on collateral flow. Generally, 'time is brain' - 1.9 million neurons die per minute of ischemia. Good collaterals can maintain penumbra for hours; poor collaterals may not.

whyChain.why: What determines core vs penumbra on imaging?
whyChain.answer: Core: severely reduced cerebral blood flow (CBF) AND cerebral blood volume (CBV) - dead tissue. Penumbra: reduced CBF but maintained CBV (tissue trying to compensate) - at risk but viable.

questions.stem: Her blood pressure is 178/95 mmHg.
questions.question: Why is permissive hypertension allowed in acute ischemic stroke (before thrombolysis)?
questions.hint: Consider cerebral perfusion pressure in the penumbra.

options.text: Hypertension is never permitted in stroke
Elevated BP maintains perfusion to the ischemic penumbra through collaterals
BP elevation is a marker of stroke severity only

questions.explanation: Cerebral autoregulation is impaired in ischemic tissue. The penumbra depends on systemic BP to maintain perfusion. Aggressive BP lowering can extend infarct. Allow BP up to 220/120 if not receiving thrombolysis.

whyChain.why: What's the target if giving tPA?
whyChain.answer: If giving thrombolysis, BP must be <185/110 before AND <180/105 for 24 hours after. Higher BP increases hemorrhagic transformation risk with tPA.

whyChain.why: What agents are used for BP control?
whyChain.answer: IV labetalol or nicardipine are first-line. Avoid nitroprusside (increases ICP). Goal is controlled, modest reduction - not aggressive lowering.

questions.stem: The patient has atrial fibrillation and is not on anticoagulation.
questions.question: What is the mechanism of stroke in atrial fibrillation?
questions.hint: Consider what happens with ineffective atrial contraction.

options.text: Stasis in left atrial appendage → thrombus formation → embolism to brain
Direct cardiac ischemia causing brain hypoperfusion

questions.explanation: In AFib, the atria fibrillate rather than contract effectively, causing blood stasis especially in the left atrial appendage. This promotes thrombus formation. Thrombi can embolize to the brain, causing stroke - typically large vessel occlusion like her MCA.

whyChain.why: Why was she not on anticoagulation?
whyChain.answer: She stopped warfarin due to bruising - a common reason for non-adherence. AFib confers 5x stroke risk; anticoagulation reduces this by 65%. Her CHA2DS2-VASc score is very high (age, female, HTN, DM, prior TIA).

whyChain.why: What's her CHA2DS2-VASc score?
whyChain.answer: Age ≥75 (2) + Female (1) + HTN (1) + DM (1) + Prior TIA (2) = 7. This predicts ~10% annual stroke risk without anticoagulation. She clearly needed to be anticoagulated.

questions.stem: Her glucose is 156 mg/dL.
questions.question: Why is hyperglycemia harmful in acute stroke?
questions.hint: Consider the metabolic environment in ischemic tissue.

options.text: Promotes anaerobic glycolysis and lactic acidosis in ischemic tissue, worsening injury

questions.explanation: Hyperglycemia in ischemic tissue promotes anaerobic metabolism with lactate accumulation, worsening acidosis and cellular injury. It's associated with larger infarcts and worse outcomes. Target glucose 140-180 mg/dL in acute stroke.

whyChain.why: Should hypoglycemia be avoided too?
whyChain.answer: Absolutely. Hypoglycemia can mimic stroke AND worsen ischemic injury. Always check glucose immediately - it's one of the few 'must know' labs before tPA. Avoid glucose <60 or >180.

questions.stem: Her stroke is classified as cardioembolic based on AFib and large vessel occlusion.
questions.question: What are the major stroke etiologies in the TOAST classification?
questions.hint: TOAST categorizes ischemic stroke by mechanism.

options.text: Large artery atherosclerosis, Cardioembolism, Small vessel occlusion, Other determined, Undetermined

questions.explanation: TOAST classification: (1) Large artery atherosclerosis (carotid stenosis), (2) Cardioembolism (AFib, valve disease), (3) Small vessel/lacunar (HTN, DM), (4) Other determined (dissection, vasculitis), (5) Undetermined (cryptogenic or multiple causes).

whyChain.why: Why does etiology matter?
whyChain.answer: Treatment differs by etiology: cardioembolism → anticoagulation; large artery → carotid intervention + antiplatelet; small vessel → risk factor control + antiplatelet; dissection → anticoagulation vs antiplatelet.

questions.stem: The CT shows early ischemic changes in the left insular cortex.
questions.question: What is the earliest CT finding in acute ischemic stroke?
questions.hint: Cytotoxic edema causes subtle density changes.

options.text: Loss of gray-white differentiation and insular ribbon sign
Large hypodense area with mass effect

questions.explanation: Early ischemic changes (within 3-6 hours): loss of gray-white differentiation, insular ribbon sign (loss of insular cortex definition), obscuration of lentiform nucleus, and subtle hypodensity. These indicate cytotoxic edema from cell death.

whyChain.why: When does CT become clearly abnormal?
whyChain.answer: CT is often normal in first 3-6 hours. Clear hypodensity develops at 6-12 hours. Mass effect and edema peak at 3-5 days. CT's main acute role is excluding hemorrhage, not confirming ischemia.

whyChain.why: Is MRI better?
whyChain.answer: Yes - diffusion-weighted MRI (DWI) shows ischemia within minutes. However, CT is faster, more available, and sufficient for tPA decisions. MRI is useful when diagnosis is uncertain.

questions.stem: Large MCA strokes can cause cerebral edema.
questions.question: When does cerebral edema peak after ischemic stroke, and what is the feared complication?
questions.hint: Edema develops over days, not hours.

options.text: Peaks at 1 hour; causes immediate herniation
Edema doesn't occur in ischemic stroke
Peaks at 3-5 days; can cause herniation ('malignant MCA syndrome')

questions.explanation: Cytotoxic edema begins immediately but vasogenic edema and maximal swelling peak at 3-5 days. Large MCA infarcts (>50% of MCA territory) can cause 'malignant cerebral edema' with midline shift and herniation - mortality >80% without decompressive craniectomy.

whyChain.why: Who should be considered for decompressive craniectomy?
whyChain.answer: Patients <60 years with malignant MCA infarct (>50% territory) within 48 hours of onset. DECIMAL, DESTINY, and HAMLET trials showed mortality reduction from ~75% to ~25%. Discuss early if large infarct.

questions.stem: The ischemic cascade leads to neuronal death in stroke.
questions.question: What is the primary mechanism of cell death in the ischemic core?
questions.hint: Without oxygen, cells cannot maintain ionic gradients.

options.text: ATP depletion → failure of Na/K-ATPase → calcium influx → excitotoxicity and necrosis

questions.explanation: Without blood flow, ATP is depleted within minutes. Na/K-ATPase fails, causing depolarization. Glutamate release causes excitotoxicity. Calcium influx triggers enzymatic destruction. This cascade leads to necrotic cell death in the core within minutes.

whyChain.why: Is the penumbra dying by the same mechanism?
whyChain.answer: The penumbra has reduced but not absent flow, so it dies more slowly through a combination of excitotoxicity, inflammation, and apoptosis. This slower death provides the window for intervention.

questions.stem: The patient presents within the potential window for thrombolysis.
questions.question: What is the time window for IV alteplase (tPA) in acute ischemic stroke?
questions.hint: The window has been extended with imaging selection.

options.text: Within 4.5 hours of last known well (or with favorable imaging up to 9 hours/wake-up stroke)

questions.explanation: Standard window is 4.5 hours from last known well. EXTEND and WAKE-UP trials showed benefit up to 9 hours (or wake-up strokes) if imaging shows salvageable tissue (perfusion mismatch or DWI-FLAIR mismatch). She was last known well at 11 PM - now 10 AM (11 hours) - outside standard window.

whyChain.why: Is she a tPA candidate?
whyChain.answer: Last known well was 11 hours ago (11 PM to 10 AM), so she's outside the 4.5-hour standard window. However, she has favorable perfusion imaging (small core, large penumbra) and may qualify for extended-window tPA or thrombectomy.

whyChain.why: What's the dose of tPA?
whyChain.answer: Alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90mg): 10% as bolus, remaining 90% infused over 60 minutes. Tenecteplase is emerging as an alternative (single bolus, non-inferior).

questions.stem: She has a large vessel occlusion (LVO) of the left M1.
questions.question: What is the treatment of choice for large vessel occlusion stroke?
questions.hint: This has revolutionized stroke care since 2015.
questions.explanation: Mechanical thrombectomy is the standard of care for LVO strokes within 24 hours if favorable imaging. Multiple trials (DAWN, DEFUSE-3) showed benefit even in extended windows with perfusion mismatch. It's superior to IV tPA alone for LVO.

whyChain.why: What's her thrombectomy eligibility?
whyChain.answer: She has LVO (M1), NIHSS 18 (severe), small core (25mL), large penumbra (85mL), and good baseline function. She's an excellent candidate despite extended time window per DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria.

whyChain.why: Should she also get tPA?
whyChain.answer: If within the tPA window and no contraindications, give tPA AND proceed to thrombectomy (don't delay thrombectomy to complete tPA infusion). She's outside standard tPA window but may qualify for extended-window tPA.

questions.question: What agent is first-line for BP reduction to enable thrombolysis?
questions.hint: Need titratable IV agent.
questions.explanation: IV labetalol (10-20mg boluses) or nicardipine infusion (5mg/hr, titrate) are first-line. They're titratable and don't increase intracranial pressure. Nitroprusside can raise ICP. Sublingual nifedipine causes unpredictable drops and is never appropriate.

questions.stem: After the acute phase, secondary prevention must be addressed.
questions.question: What anticoagulant is preferred for secondary stroke prevention in AFib?
questions.hint: DOACs have largely replaced warfarin.

options.text: Direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) - e.g., apixaban, rivaroxaban

questions.explanation: DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are preferred over warfarin for AFib-related stroke prevention. They have similar or better efficacy, lower bleeding risk (especially intracranial), no INR monitoring, and fewer interactions.

whyChain.why: When should anticoagulation start after stroke?
whyChain.answer: Timing depends on stroke size: small infarct - start in 1-3 days; moderate - 3-7 days; large - 7-14 days. Risk is hemorrhagic transformation. No anticoagulation until repeat imaging confirms no hemorrhage.

whyChain.why: Why did she stop warfarin before?
whyChain.answer: She stopped due to bruising - a common issue. DOACs may be more acceptable (fewer interactions, no monitoring). Address her concerns and emphasize the 10% annual stroke risk without anticoagulation.

questions.hint: Recent trials support aggressive lipid lowering.
questions.explanation: High-intensity statin therapy targeting LDL <70 mg/dL (or >50% reduction) is recommended for secondary prevention. TST and SPARCL trials support aggressive LDL lowering. Some guidelines now target <55 mg/dL for very high-risk patients.

questions.stem: You're calculating her NIH Stroke Scale (NIHSS).
questions.question: Her NIHSS is 18. What does this indicate about stroke severity?
questions.hint: NIHSS ranges from 0-42.

options.text: Severe stroke (NIHSS >15 indicates severe deficit and likely LVO)

questions.explanation: NIHSS stratifies severity: 0 = no deficit, 1-4 = minor, 5-15 = moderate, 16-20 = severe, >20 = very severe. Her NIHSS 18 indicates severe stroke. NIHSS ≥6 strongly suggests LVO requiring thrombectomy evaluation.

whyChain.why: What does NIHSS measure?
whyChain.answer: 11 items: level of consciousness (3 questions), gaze, visual fields, facial palsy, arm/leg motor (each side), ataxia, sensory, language, dysarthria, extinction/inattention. Weighted toward anterior circulation deficits.

whyChain.why: Are there limitations?
whyChain.answer: NIHSS underweights posterior circulation (brainstem/cerebellar) strokes. A patient can have devastating basilar occlusion with relatively low NIHSS. Also doesn't capture all cognitive deficits.

questions.question: What does TICI 2b/3 reperfusion indicate?
questions.hint: TICI grades angiographic reperfusion success.
questions.explanation: TICI (Thrombolysis in Cerebral Infarction) scale: 0 = no perfusion, 1 = minimal, 2a = partial (<50%), 2b = substantial (>50%), 3 = complete. TICI 2b-3 is considered successful reperfusion and is the goal of thrombectomy.

whyChain.why: What's her expected outcome?
whyChain.answer: With successful reperfusion, 50-60% of patients achieve functional independence (mRS 0-2) vs ~20% with medical therapy alone. Earlier reperfusion = better outcomes.

questions.stem: You're counseling her husband about prognosis.
questions.question: What is the biggest determinant of stroke outcome?
questions.hint: Think about what's modifiable in acute management.
questions.explanation: Time from onset to reperfusion is the strongest modifiable predictor. Every 15-minute delay to thrombectomy results in measurably worse outcomes. The phrase 'time is brain' reflects that 1.9 million neurons die per minute of ischemia.

whyChain.why: What other factors affect prognosis?
whyChain.answer: Baseline function, stroke severity (NIHSS), age, comorbidities, infarct volume, reperfusion success, and complications (hemorrhage, edema, infections). Early rehabilitation also impacts recovery.

questions.stem: 24 hours after thrombectomy, the patient becomes more lethargic with new headache.
questions.question: Repeat CT shows hyperdensity in the infarcted area. What complication occurred?
questions.hint: This is a feared complication of reperfusion.
questions.explanation: Hemorrhagic transformation occurs when damaged blood vessels in infarcted tissue leak after reperfusion. It ranges from petechial (often asymptomatic) to parenchymal hematoma with mass effect (symptomatic, worse prognosis). Risk factors: large infarct, reperfusion therapy, anticoagulation.

whyChain.why: How is it managed?
whyChain.answer: Stop anticoagulation/antiplatelets, reverse if on tPA (give cryoprecipitate/TXA for fibrinogen), control BP strictly, consider neurosurgical evacuation if large with deterioration, and repeat imaging to assess progression.

whyChain.why: Does this mean thrombolysis was wrong?
whyChain.answer: Not necessarily - hemorrhagic transformation can occur even without reperfusion therapy. The overall benefit of reperfusion outweighs bleeding risk in appropriately selected patients. This is why patient selection is critical.

questions.hint: Consider complications of dysphagia and immobility.
questions.explanation: Aspiration pneumonia is the most common infection after stroke, occurring in 10-30% of patients. questions.stem: On day 4, the patient develops worsening headache and decreased consciousness.
questions.question: CT shows significant midline shift. What is the management for malignant cerebral edema?
questions.hint: Medical and surgical options exist.

options.text: Consider decompressive hemicraniectomy if age <60 and within 48 hours

questions.explanation: Decompressive hemicraniectomy reduces mortality from ~75% to ~25% in malignant MCA syndrome. Best outcomes in patients <60 years, within 48 hours, before herniation. Medical measures (osmotic therapy, head elevation) are temporizing. Steroids are NOT effective for cytotoxic edema.

whyChain.why: What about older patients?
whyChain.answer: DESTINY II showed mortality benefit even in patients 61-82, but with more disability. Decision involves goals of care discussions - survival may be with significant dependence.

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with optic neuritis. MRI brain shows 4 periventricular white matter lesions. CSF shows oligoclonal bands. Which criteria are met for MS diagnosis?

options.text: Both DIS and DIT are met — diagnose MS now
options.explanation: DIS is met but DIT requires evidence of lesions at different time points. The initial scan alone (even with oligoclonal bands) cannot establish DIT unless both enhancing AND non-enhancing lesions are present simultaneously.

options.text: CSF oligoclonal bands alone establish the diagnosis regardless of MRI findings
options.explanation: Oligoclonal bands support the diagnosis and can substitute for DIT in certain scenarios per 2017 McDonald criteria, but they do not independently establish the diagnosis without meeting DIS.

options.text: Neither criterion is met — optic neuritis is a clinically isolated syndrome only
options.explanation: While optic neuritis alone is a CIS, the 4 periventricular white matter lesions establish DIS. CIS + DIS constitutes significant risk for MS.

options.text: Dissemination in space (DIS) is met; need evidence of dissemination in time (DIT) for definitive diagnosis
options.explanation: Per the 2017 McDonald criteria, DIS requires ≥1 T2 lesion in ≥2 of 4 MS-typical regions (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord). With periventricular lesions + optic neuritis (separate CNS region), DIS is met. DIT can be met by a new T2 or gadolinium-enhancing lesion on follow-up MRI, or simultaneously by both enhancing and non-enhancing lesions.

imaging_reasoning.order_reasoning: Non-contrast CT to EXCLUDE hemorrhage (within minutes); CTA for LVO; perfusion for mismatch
